content:
[S1]: The Earth goes around the Sun in a big circle every year.  
[READER]: Hmm, I know the Earth moves, but why does it matter? I want to know what that has to do with seasons! The next sentence should explain why moving around the Sun changes the seasons.  

[S2]: The Earth is tilted a little bit, like when you lean to one side.  
[READER]: Oh, so the Earth isn’t straight? That’s interesting! Now I want to know how this tilt changes things. The next sentence should tell me what happens because of this tilt.  

[S3]: Because of the tilt, sometimes one part of the Earth gets more sunlight, and sometimes it gets less.  
[READER]: Oh! So the sunlight changes because of the tilt? That makes sense! But why does more sunlight mean summer? The next sentence should explain that.  

[S4]: When a place gets more sunlight, it gets warmer, and that’s called summer there.  
[READER]: I get it! More sunlight means warmer weather and summer. But what about when there’s less sunlight? The next sentence should talk about that.  

[S5]: When that place gets less sunlight, it gets colder, and that’s called winter.  
[READER]: So sunlight makes it hot or cold? That’s cool! But what about spring and fall? What happens then? The next sentence should explain the other seasons.  

[S6]: Spring and fall happen when the Earth is in between, so places get about the same sunlight as usual.  
[READER]: Oh, so spring and fall are like a mix of warm and cold? That’s neat! I want to know if this happens all over the world the same way. The next sentence should explain if seasons are the same everywhere.  

[S7]: Actually, when it’s summer in one part of the Earth, it’s winter on the other part because the tilt points one side toward the Sun and the other side away.  
[READER]: Wow, so when I’m having summer, kids far away could be having winter? That’s surprising! I want a little summary now to remember all this. The next sentence should wrap it up nicely.  

[S8]: So, the seasons happen because the Earth moves around the Sun and is tilted, making different parts warmer or colder during the year.  
[READER]: That makes sense now! I can imagine the Earth leaning and moving around the Sun to make the seasons change. Thanks for explaining it!

===BEGIN===  
The Earth goes around the Sun in a big circle every year.  
The Earth is tilted a little bit, like when you lean to one side.  
Because of the tilt, sometimes one part of the Earth gets more sunlight, and sometimes it gets less.  
When a place gets more sunlight, it gets warmer, and that’s called summer there.  
When that place gets less sunlight, it gets colder, and that’s called winter.  
Spring and fall happen when the Earth is in between, so places get about the same sunlight as usual.  
Actually, when it’s summer in one part of the Earth, it’s winter on the other part because the tilt points one side toward the Sun and the other side away.  
So, the seasons happen because the Earth moves around the Sun and is tilted, making different parts warmer or colder during the year.  
===END===
